title: Tryed Side Table
summary: A compact pocket-hole side table with chunky 4x4 legs, a breadboard-ended 2x6 top, and 2x4 aprons — the small-scale cousin of the Tryde table family.
description:
This is the little sibling in the "Tryde" family of tables — the same breadboard-end tabletop construction and pocket-hole apron base as the larger console and coffee tables, scaled down to end-table size. Build the tabletop first as its own flat assembly (center boards, then breadboard ends), then build the base separately, so you're never fighting to keep a large glued-up top square while also chasing leg alignment.

Drive the pocket holes in the aprons before assembly, and pay attention to which face they're on — the plan's whole final step depends on those apron pocket holes already pointing up so the base can be screwed straight into the underside of the finished top.

steps.title: Join the tabletop center pieces
steps.body: Drill 1-1/2" pocket holes along one long edge of two of the 2x6 boards, then attach all four 2x6 center pieces together with 2-1/2" pocket hole screws and glue.

steps.title: Attach the breadboard ends
steps.body: Measure and cut the 2x4 breadboard ends, then attach them to the joined 2x6 center panel with 2-1/2" pocket hole screws.

steps.title: Prepare the aprons
steps.body: Cut the four aprons to length and drill 1-1/2" pocket holes into each end.

steps.title: Attach the aprons to the legs
steps.body: Attach the aprons to the legs with 2-1/2" pocket hole screws, making sure the pocket holes meant for attaching the top are oriented upward.

steps.title: Attach the base to the tabletop
steps.body: Attach the base to the underside of the tabletop through the apron pocket holes with 2-1/2" pocket hole screws. Sand and finish with stain and topcoat.

images.alt: A small side table with chunky 4x4 legs and a breadboard-ended 2x6 top
